narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 33, you've put together a thoughtful application for law school, including your plans to attend full-time. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 51% chance that you would fail it. This tipped the prediction toward failure, leading to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger signals of bar success. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's rank around the middle of the pack. Coming in second was your low family income bracket, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward passing, unlike higher-income peers who sometimes face different pressures. Your Year 1 decile ranking of 1 also contributed toward the failure side, though less strongly than your later performance. Compared to average applicants—who often rank around the 5th or 6th decile in both early and later years—your lower early rankings stood out as red flags for long-term bar success. Your LSAT and GPA were close to typical, so they didn't sway things much either way. While your full-time status aligns with most applicants, it didn't offset the key concerns. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest background; it's the model highlighting patterns from past students where early and mid-school struggles correlated with bar challenges. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above the average 36.7, strengthening your academic profile through additional coursework or test prep, and reapplying next cycle with updated decile rankings if possible—many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps.
